policy_statement: Execute an agreement with the U.S. Department of Energy's National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) to pursue mutually beneficial projects to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_description: Execute an agreement with NREL to pursue projects that reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance collaboration/R&D support → improved planning/technology deployment → ↓ fossil energy use / ↓ process emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: A local government formalizes a partnership with a research/technical institution (e.g., NREL) to provide analysis, modeling, technical assistance, pilot projects, and project development support.  
(2) Mediating process: Better data, technical capacity, and project design reduces barriers (information, financing readiness, performance uncertainty), improving adoption and performance of clean energy, efficiency, electrification, or other emissions-reducing interventions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced greenhouse gas emissions (CO₂e) from energy supply and end-use systems due to accelerated/expanded deployment of mitigation projects.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is enabling or accelerating implementation of measures that reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The partnership is explicitly oriented toward projects that reduce GHG emissions; the principal climate effect is mitigation via enabling emissions-reducing actions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While governance and innovation capacity can also improve resilience or resource efficiency, the stated purpose and expected largest climate outcome here is emissions reduction (Mitigation), not hazard exposure reduction (Adaptation), not “same service with less energy/material input” as the primary endpoint (Resource Efficiency), and not ecosystem enhancement (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the partnership’s projects were primarily adaptation-focused (e.g., sea-level rise planning) the category could shift; if it were mainly about efficiency it could resemble Resource Efficiency, but under this canonical mechanism the unifying intent is GHG reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any interagency research/technical partnership framed as pursuing projects to reduce GHG emissions shares the same dominant pathway—enabled interventions leading to lower emissions—so Mitigation applies generally.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: The specific mitigation sub-type (renewables, efficiency, electrification, methane, etc.) depends on the actual projects pursued and is not determined at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Enabling/coordination mechanisms can be “indirect”: if agreements produce only studies with no implementation, realized mitigation may be minimal; also, if the partnership’s actual project portfolio is adaptation- or resilience-dominant, the primary category would change, but that would be a different canonical mechanism than “→ emissions_reduction_pathway”.
key_indicators: "Execute an agreement"; "National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL)"; "pursue mutually beneficial projects"; "reduce greenhouse gas emissions"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: local air quality improvements, energy cost savings, workforce/innovation gains (not explicit enough to classify as separate categories).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a procurement/contracting action (agreement for services/technical assistance), but the text frames it primarily as an inter-institutional collaboration to pursue projects, fitting planning/strategy more cleanly.
